perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” means all confidential and proprietary information belonging to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) that is revealed to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) during the Disclosure Period, which begins on the effective date and ends April 26, 2011. Confidential Information, regardless of the form in which it appears, may include, without limitation, information about customers, business and marketing plans, financial data, expertise of employees, forecasts and strategies that is related to the business of the Disclosing Party and is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Confidential Information” means all information, regardless of its proprietary nature, belonging to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) that is revealed to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) during the Disclosure Period, which begins on the effective date and ends April 26, 2011. Confidential Information, regardless of the form in which it appears, may include, without limitation, information about customers, business and marketing plans, financial data, expertise of employees, forecasts and strategies that is related to the business of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'confidential and proprietary' qualifier, the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes overly broad and undefined.  This weakens the protection of truly confidential information and introduces ambiguity about what is actually protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret by including all information regardless of whether it derives independent economic value from not being generally known, or readily ascertainable through proper means. The federal trade secrets act requires that the information be confidential, derive independent economic value, and be subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the 'confidential and proprietary' qualifier. The Law Explanation indicates this contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, particularly concerning information deriving independent economic value from being secret. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1839, defines 'trade secret' and includes the requirement that '(B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known...'. By removing 'confidential and proprietary', the contract no longer ensures that the information protected meets the legal standard of a trade secret, thus creating a contradiction because the contract now includes information that does not meet the legal definition of a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using a degree of care deemed appropriate by its internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the 'reasonable degree of care' standard with a standard based on the Receiving Party's internal policies. This could be significantly lower than what would be considered 'reasonable' under law, particularly in cases involving sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act which requires 'reasonable' efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Allowing internal policies to dictate the level of care could fall short of the 'reasonable' standard, making it harder to claim trade secret misappropriation.

Many states in the U.S. have adopted the act. Massachusetts, New Jersey and New York adopted it in 2010. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the 'reasonable degree of care' standard required to protect confidential information, replacing it with a standard determined by the Receiving Party's internal policies. This directly contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act's requirement for 'reasonable' efforts to maintain secrecy, as allowing internal policies to dictate the level of care could fall short of this standard. The scraped law snippet 2 references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, stating it provides states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry. By replacing the 'reasonable' standard with an internal policy standard, the modified contract undermines this legal framework, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information, together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will make a good faith effort to return or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'promptly return or destroy' with 'make a good faith effort' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The original text provides a clear directive. The modified version allows for excuses and delays, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA), 17 U.S. Code § 1201
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, if the 'Confidential Information' includes copyrighted material protected by technological measures (e.g., software, digital files), failure to promptly destroy the material could be construed as circumventing copyright protection measures, as continued possession may enable unauthorized access or copying, depending on the specific technological measure in place and the access controls. The 'good faith effort' standard would not be sufficient to meet the obligations required to avoid a DMCA violation. A 'good faith effort' could mean the Receiving Party does nothing but say they tried.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 1201 - Circumvention of copyright protection systems | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 1201 - Circumvention of copyright protection systems U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Violations Regarding Circumvention of Technological Measures.— (1) (A) No person shall circumvent a technological measure that effectively controls access to a work protected under this title. The prohibition contained in the preceding sentence shall take effect at the end of the 2-year period beginning on the date of the enactment of this chapter. (B) The prohibition contained in subparagraph (A) shall not apply to persons who are users of a copyrighted work which is in a particular class of works, if such persons are, or are likely to be in the succeeding 3-year period, adversely affected by virtue of such prohibition in their ability to make noninfringing uses of that particular class of works under this title, as determined under subparagraph (C). (C) During the 2-year period described in subparagraph (A), and during each succeeding 3-year period, the Librarian of Congress , upon the recommendation of the Register of Copyrights, who shall consult with the Assistant Secretary for Communications and Information of the Department of Commerce and report and comment on his or her views in making such recommendation, shall make the determination in a rulemaking proceeding for purposes of subparagraph (B) of whether persons who are users of a copyrighted work are, or are likely to be in the succeeding 3-year period, adversely affected by the prohibition under subparagraph (A) in their ability to make noninfringing uses under this title of a particular class of copyrighted works. In conducting such rulemaking, the Librarian shall examine— (i) the availability for use of copyrighted works; (ii) the availability for use of works for nonprofit archival, preservation, and education
perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly return or destroy' to 'make a good faith effort' weakens the obligation concerning confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests that if the 'Confidential Information' includes copyrighted material protected by technological measures, the 'good faith effort' standard would not be sufficient to meet the obligations required to avoid a DMCA violation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, both referencing 17 U.S. Code § 1201, address the circumvention of copyright protection systems. The original text ensures a clear directive, while the modified version introduces ambiguity, potentially enabling unauthorized access or copying of copyrighted material, and is therefore a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party’s obligations imposed pursuant to this Agreement will continue for two years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party’s obligations imposed pursuant to this Agreement will continue at the Disclosing Party’s discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the fixed two-year term to a term determined at the discretion of the Disclosing Party. This creates an open-ended obligation that can be considered unreasonable and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as described in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205. Giving one party complete discretion over the duration of the agreement allows for arbitrary and unfair enforcement, potentially harming the Receiving Party's business opportunities beyond what is reasonably necessary to protect the Confidential Information. The 'at will' termination removes the guarantee of mutual understanding and reliance that underpins contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction by changing the fixed two-year term to a term at the Disclosing Party's discretion, which violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and "observance of fair dealing standards." Granting one party complete discretion over the duration of the agreement contradicts the standard of fair dealing, as it allows for arbitrary enforcement and potentially harms the Receiving Party's business opportunities. The original contract guaranteed a two-year period. The changed text eliminates this guarantee, contradicting the concept of 'honest dealing' since the duration can change at any moment at the Disclosing Party's discretion.
